pimpQuestions: Q: What commonly associated injury, if missed, may compromise ACL reconstruction? A: Posterolateral corner injury

Q: What landmarks may be used for placement of the tibial tunnel during ACL reconstruction? A: 1 cm anterior to the PCL, Slightly anterior to the medial tibial eminence, In line with the posterior border of the anterior horn of the lateral meniscus
Q: What is the most common cause of ACL reconstruction failure? A: Tunnel malpositioning
Q: What complication occurs if the femoral tunnel is positioned too anteriorly? A: Knee will be tight in flexion and loose in extension
Q: What complication occurs if the tibial tunnel is positioned too anteriorly? A: Graft impingement in extension
Q: How is the femoral tunnel most commonly malpositioned during ACL reconstruction? A: Too anterior
Q: What is a cyclops lesion? A: Localized fibrosis of the native ACL stump following reconstruction
Q: What is the drawback of using a BTB graft for ACL reconstruction? A: Increased anterior knee pain
Q: What is the typical pattern of bone bruising seen on MRI following an ACL tear? A: Anterior or middle 1/3 of the lateral femoral condyle, Posterior 1/3 of the lateral tibial plateau
Q: Where should the femoral tunnel be positioned during ACL reconstruction? A: 9-10 O'clock position, 6-8 mm from posterior cortex
Q: The knee should be held in what position when tensioning an ACL graft? A: Approximately 20° of flexion with a reverse Lachman motion
otherUsefulFacts: The bundles of the ACL are Anteromedial and Posterolateral.
The anterior drawer primarily tests the Anteromedial bundle of the ACL.
The pivot shift primarily tests the Posterolateral bundle of the ACL.
The most specific physical exam test for an ACL rupture is the Pivot shift.
The two most commonly used grafts for ACL reconstruction are Bone-Patellar Tendon-Bone (BTB) and Quadruple hamstring (Gracilis and Semitendinosus).
The benefit of using a BTB graft for ACL reconstruction is bone to bone healing.

selected.rationale: Standard approach for ACL reconstruction providing direct access to the knee joint and ACL.

approachSelection.notes: Only one anatomically appropriate approach is available in the catalog for ACL reconstruction.

questions.q: Which nerve branch is at risk during the medial parapatellar approach to the knee?
questions.answer: Infrapatellar branch of the saphenous nerve

questions.q: Through which muscular interval is the medial parapatellar approach performed?
questions.answer: Rectus femoris and vastus medialis interval

questions.q: Which retinaculum is incised during the medial parapatellar approach to the knee?
Name the bones exposed during the anterior medial parapatellar approach to the knee.
What joint is accessed via the medial parapatellar surgical approach?
The medial parapatellar approach involves incision adjacent to which border of the patella?
Which muscles are separated or retracted to gain access in the medial parapatellar approach?
Is the infrapatellar branch of the saphenous nerve typically spared or at risk during the medial parapatellar approach?
